coredata.dc:title: Highly dispersed few-layer MoS2 nanosheets on S, N co-doped carbon for electrocatalytic H2 production
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2017 Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, the Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, the Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Shixin
Qu, Dan
An, Li
Xi, Guangcheng
Chen, Ge
Li, Fan
Zhou, Zhijun
Sun, Zaicheng
coredata.dc:description: Ultrathin small MoS2 nanosheets exhibit a higher electrocatalytic activity for the hydrogen evolution reaction. However, strong interactions between MoS2 layers may result in aggregation; together with the low conductivity of MoS2, this may lower its electrocatalytic activity. In this paper we present a method that we developed to directly produce solid S, N co-doped carbon (SNC) with a graphite structure and multiple surface groups through a hydrothermal route. When Na2MoO4 was added to the reaction, polymolybdate could be anchored into the carbon materials via a chemical interaction that helps polymolybdate disperse uniformly into the SNC. After a high temperature treatment, polymolybdate transformed into MoS2 at 800 °C for 6 h in a N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C/min, owing to S2– being released from the SNC during the treatment (denoted as MoS2/SNC-800-6h). The SNC effectively prevents MoS2 from aggregating into large particles, and we successfully prepared highly dispersed MoS2 in the SNC matrix. Electrochemical characterizations indicate that MoS2/SNC-900-12h exhibits a low onset potential of 115 mV and a low overpotential of 237 mV at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. Furthermore, MoS2/SNC-900-12h also had an excellent stability with only ∼2.6% decay at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 after 5000 test cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2 nanosheet
S, N co-doped carbon
Electrocatalytic hydrogen production
Composite
Hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of SNC (a); PMS/SNC (b); SNC-900-12h (c); and MoS2/SNC-900-12h (d).
TME (a) and HRTEM (b) images of MoS2/SNC-900-12h.

(a) XRD patterns of bulk MoS2, SNC-900-12h, and MoS2/SNC-900-12h; (b) XRD patterns of MoS2/SNC-T at various annealing temperatures and annealing durations; (c) XRD patterns of MoS2/SNC-900-12h with various Na2MoO2·2H2O loadings; (d) Raman spectra of SNC-900-12h and MoS2/SNC-900-12h.

XPS survey spectra of the SNC-900-12h (1) and MoS2/SNC-900-12h composites (2) (a); High-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of SNC-900-12h (b) and MoS2/SNC-900-12h (c); High-resolution XPS spectra of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h Mo 3d and S 2s (d); S 2p (e); and N 1s (f).

(a) HER polarization curves and (b) the corresponding Tafel plots of 20% Pt/C, bulk MoS2, SNC-900-12h, and MoS2/SNC-900-12h recorded on GCE with a catalyst loading of 0.285 mg/cm2 at a potential scan rate of 5 mV/s in H2SO4 solution (0.5 mol/L); (c) HER polarization curves of 20% Pt/C and MoS2/SNC-T-t; (d) HER polarization curves of 20% Pt/C and MoS2/SNC-900-12h with addition of Na2MoO42H2O.

(a) Electrochemical impedance spectra of bulk MoS2, SNC-900-12h, and MoS2/SNC-900-12h composites in HER at –0.20 V (vs. RHE); (b) Equivalent circuit of MoS2/SNC-900-12h; (c) Electrochemical impedance spectra of MoS2/SNC-T-t in HER at –0.20 V (vs. RHE); (d) Electrochemical impedance spectra of MoS2/SNC-900-12h with addition of Na2MoO42H2O in HER at –0.20 V (vs. RHE).

(a) Stability test for the MoS2/SNC-900-12h recorded on GCE with a catalyst loading of 1 mg/cm2. A small degradation of HER activity is observed after 5000 cycles between 0 and –0.3 V (vs. RHE) at a potential scan rate of 100 mV/s in H2SO4 solution (0.5 mol/L); (b) XRD patterns of MoS2/SNC-900-12h before and after 5000 cycles; (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of MoS2/SNC-900-12h after 5000 cycles.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic process for the generation of the MoS2/SNC-T-t composites.
The elemental analysis of SNC and SNC-900-12h.
Impedance parameters determined from fitting the EIS responses of the various catalysts.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21671011) and Beijing High-Level Talent program.

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: Article (Special Issue on Nanoscience and Catalysis)

head.ce:title: Highly dispersed few-layer MoS2 nanosheets on S, N co-doped carbon for electrocatalytic H2 production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis and Separation, College of Environmental and Energy Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Chaoyang Distrcti, Beijing 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis and Separation
College of Environmental and Energy Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications, Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics, and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130033, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials and Nanoproducts Research Center, Chinese Academy of Inspection and Quarantine, Beijing 100000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chinese Academy of Inspection and Quarantine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nuclear Physics and Chemistry, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621000, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nuclear Physics and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrathin small MoS2 nanosheets exhibit a higher electrocatalytic activity for the hydrogen evolution reaction. However, strong interactions between MoS2 layers may result in aggregation; together with the low conductivity of MoS2, this may lower its electrocatalytic activity. In this paper we present a method that we developed to directly produce solid S, N co-doped carbon (SNC) with a graphite structure and multiple surface groups through a hydrothermal route. When Na2MoO4 was added to the reaction, polymolybdate could be anchored into the carbon materials via a chemical interaction that helps polymolybdate disperse uniformly into the SNC. After a high temperature treatment, polymolybdate transformed into MoS2 at 800 °C for 6 h in a N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C/min, owing to S2– being released from the SNC during the treatment (denoted as MoS2/SNC-800-6h). The SNC effectively prevents MoS2 from aggregating into large particles, and we successfully prepared highly dispersed MoS2 in the SNC matrix. Electrochemical characterizations indicate that MoS2/SNC-900-12h exhibits a low onset potential of 115 mV and a low overpotential of 237 mV at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. Furthermore, MoS2/SNC-900-12h also had an excellent stability with only ∼2.6% decay at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 after 5000 test cycles.

Highly dispersed few-layer MoS2 nanosheets on S, N co-doped carbon were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal, high temperature annealing route. More catalytic active sites were thus exposed and the electrocatalytic properties were greatly enhanced for the hydrogen evolution reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen energy is clean and one of the most promising candidates for replacing fossil fuels in the future [1]. The electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is considered to be one of the most common and efficient pathways to produce H2. Currently, the most efficient HER electrocatalysts are noble metals such as Pt [2,3]. However, the high price and limited reserve of noble metals inhibit their large scale application in practice. Thus, developing a low cost, highly efficient electrocatalyst is urgently needed to progress sustainable energy infrastructure [4–8].

Over the past few years, MoS2-based electrocatalysts have attracted increased research attention because they are low-cost, highly efficient alternatives to Pt [9,10]. Both experimental and theoretical studies have confirmed that the HER activity of MoS2 strongly depends on the number of catalytically active edge sites [11–13]. Therefore, developing MoS2 electrocatalysts with both abundant active edge sites and good crystallinity is an efficient way to simultaneously achieve high a HER performance and long-term stability of the catalyst. Jaramillo and coworkers [11] reported a highly ordered double-gyroid MoS2 bicontinuous network with preferentially exposed active edge sites that led to an outstanding improvement in HER performance. Recently, Cui's group [14] prepared MoS2 films with vertically aligned layers; this geometry allows the edges to be maximally exposed on the film's surface. Meanwhile, Xie's group [15] synthesized MoS2 ultrathin nanosheets with controllable defects leading to the exposure of additional active edge sites.

To improve the HER activity of MoS2, two aspects should be considered. First, strong van der Waals interactions between the lamellar crystals will result in aggregation, which decreases the number of active sites as well as the overall electrocatalytic activity [16]. This means that larger, multilayer MoS2 nanosheets cannot provide enough active sites. Second, the poor conductivity of MoS2, which is caused by the lateral transfer of electrons along the lamella structure of the MoS2 nanosheets, restricts the efficient transfer of electrons as well as hindering related electrochemical kinetic processes [17]. Therefore, designing MoS2 catalysts with few layers that are small and can be highly dispersed would be an effective strategy to generate more active sites, thus enhancing the electrocatalytic HER. A good electrocatalyst would thus be nanoscale MoS2 crystallites supported on carbon-based materials, since those are conducting but otherwise inert [18–20]. Recently, Rao et al. [21] prepared a MoS2 composite with nitrogen doped reduced graphite oxide, which showed excellent HER activity; this implies that nitrogen incorporated graphene should improve the catalytic activity of their composite because N doped graphene has an enhanced ability to donate electrons. N-doped carbon materials have a high electronic conductivity owing to their p-delocalization and high thermal conductivity.

Taking these factors into account, carbon materials are ideal supports to improve electrocatalytic activity. However, MoS2 nanosheets are difficult to grow on traditional carbon nanofibers owing to the inert carbon surface, which has few anchor sites on which the MoS2 can bond. Here, we present a new synthesis route that we developed to produce carbon nanostructures with high content of N and S, which can thus anchor MoO4 2– onto the carbon nanostructures. A subsequent high temperature treatment causes S2– to be released from the carbon, thus transforming MoO4 2– into MoS2. Owing to the confinement of the carbon materials, the as-formed MoS2 has only a few layers, large active sites, and is highly dispersed in the carbon matrix. We demonstrate that this MoS2/SNC composite is a good catalyst for electrochemical hydrogen evolution as it exhibits a low overpotential and high stability.

Na2MoO4·2H2O was purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, GR, 95.0–98.0%) and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, AR) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. Isopropyl alcohol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N, N-diphenyl thiourea (DPU, 99%) was obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA) anhydrous (≥99.8%) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. Platinum catalyst (20 wt% Pt/C) and nafion solution (5 wt%) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. α-Aluminum oxide polishing powder (≥99%, d = 0.5 μm and d = 50 nm), Pt foil, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl), and glassy carbon electrodes were purchased from Tianjin Aidahengsheng Technology. All chemicals were used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ/cm.

Typically, 0.242 g (1 mmol) Na2MoO4·2H2O, 0.192 g (1 mmol) CA and 0.685 g (3 mmol) DPU were dispersed into 5 mL of DMSO. After the solution was dispersed via ultrasonication for about 1 h, it was transferred into a 10 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 14 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, a polymolybdate salt (PMS)/SNC solid rod was collected, which was subsequently immersed into DI water and acetone to remove any small molecules and inorganic salts from it; this sample was dried in air at 60 °C overnight. The obtained solid powder was dark green. The powder was thermally treated in a conventional tube furnace at 700–950 °C for either 6 or 12 h in a N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C/min, thereby obtaining MoS2/SNC-T-t powder, where T stands for treatment temperature and t for treatment time. SNC-T-t was also prepared for comparison under the same conditions but in the absence of Na2MoO4·2H2O.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out using a PANalyticalX'Pert instrument. The samples were prepared by dropping the catalyst powders onto glass substrates. The Raman spectra of the powder samples were recorded on a LabRAM HR Raman microscope with a laser excitation wavelength of 514.53 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected on a SSI S-Probe XPS spectrometer. The XPS samples were prepared by casting an isopropyl alcohol suspension of the catalyst powders onto Si wafers. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi US8010. The SEM samples were prepared by casting an isopropyl alcohol suspension of the catalyst powders onto Si wafers. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 TEM instrument. Samples were prepared by dropping a diluted suspension of the catalyst powders in isopropyl alcohol onto copper grids covered with porous carbon films.

The electrochemical measurements were performed in a H2SO4 solution (0.5 mol/L) using an Autolab electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTAT302N). A graphite rod was used as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode, and the catalyst powders casted onto the glassy carbon electrode were used as the working electrode. The glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) used each had a diameter of 5 mm. They were polished using alpha alumina powder (0.5 µm and 50 nm in size) suspended in DI water on a Nylon polishing pad. To prepare the working electrode, the catalyst (4 mg) and 5 wt% nafion solution (80 µL) were dispersed in isopropyl alcohol (920 µL) and sonicated for at least 1 h to form a homogeneous mixture. Then, the mixture (14 µL) was drop casted onto the glassy carbon electrode and left to dry at room temperature. The catalyst loading density was determined to be 0.285 mg/cm2.

The H2SO4 solution (0.5 mol/L) was purged with N2 for 1 h to completely remove O2 prior to the experiment. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were recorded between 0 and –0.6 V versus Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. CVs were scanned for 50 cycles. Linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs) were measured from 0 to –0.8 V versus Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. Tafel slopes were calculated from the LSV curves by plotting the overpotential (η) against the logarithm of the current density, j. Accelerated stability tests were performed in H2SO4 solution (0.5 mol/L) for 5000 cycles with potential cycling between 0 and –0.3 V versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) and a sweep rate of 100 mV/s. At the end of the cycling test, the electrode was used to investigate the polarization curves at a sweep rate of 5 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted in the same configuration at η = –0.2 V from 106–10−2 Hz with an AC voltage of 5 mV. All the potentials were calibrated with the RHE using the equation E vs. RHE = E vs. Ag/AgCl + E Ag/AgCl + 0.059 pH. All data were iR-corrected.

It is well-documented that CA and urea or thiourea can be used to produce graphite carbon dots under hydrothermal conditions [22,23]. Here, urea was replaced by DPU to increase the conjugation of the sp 2 domain. When the reaction time was less than 12 h, carbon dots were produced in DMSO solutions. To our surprise, when CA and DPU in DMSO was transferred into an autoclave at 180 °C for 14 h, a yellowish-brown solid was obtained. After the hydrothermal reaction, all the reactants turned solid, indicating that DMSO also took part in the carbonization process to form carbon materials with high S and N content. Element analysis results indicate that the N and S content reached 7.0% and 17.2%, respectively (Table 1 ). Fig. 1(a) shows a SEM image of the as-prepared SNC after the hydrothermal reaction. It shows a solid rod that is composed of particles with diameters of 200–300 nm. Those results imply that this route could be an effective one for obtaining high N and S doped carbon materials.

To fabricate the MoS2/SNC composite, Na2MoO4 was added into the reaction as a Mo source. Typically, 1 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O, 1 mmol CA, and 3 mmol DPU were dispersed in 5 mL of DMSO. Since Na2MoO4 dissolves in DMSO at high temperatures, MoO4 2– can react with the –NH2 groups on the carbon dots produced during the initial stage of the reaction to form PMS, which helps the Mo to disperse uniformly in the solution as well as helping the carbon materials formed later to disperse. After the hydrothermal reaction, a dark green solid rod was obtained. The synthesis route of MoS2/SNC-T-t is shown in Scheme 1 . Fig. 1(b) displays a SEM image of the PMS/SNC composite. The morphology of the composite shows that nanoparticle aggregates remain after the addition of PMS. The SEM image further reveals that the solid rod is composed of particles with diameters of 100–200 nm. Then we treated the PMS/SNC composite at a high temperature to convert the PMS to MoS2 owing to the S2– and NH3 released from the SNC at high temperature. Fig. 1(c) and (d) show SEM images of the SNC and PMS/SNC after calcination at 900 °C for 12 h; both samples exhibit particle aggregates with reduced particle sizes (∼100 and ∼50 nm, respectively). Fig. 2(a) and (b) shows TEM images of MoS2/SNC-900-12h at different magnifications. The TEM images reveal that the samples consisted of highly dispersed and ultrafine MoS2 nanosheets with few layers, which massively increases the availability of catalytic edge sites. A high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2(b)) shows that graphite lattice fringes can be observed in the background, indicating that the as-prepared carbon materials are graphite phase.

Fig. 3(a) shows the XRD patterns of MoS2/SNC-900-12h, SNC-900-12h, PMS/SNC, and bulk MoS2. The XRD patterns have two diffraction peaks at 22.8° and 43.7°, which are related to the (002) and (101) peaks of the graphite of the SNC-900-12h sample [24]. This indicates that the SNC is in the graphite phase after the high temperature treatment. For PMS/SNC, no obvious diffraction peaks related to MoS2 are observed besides the carbon diffraction peaks. Fig. 3(a) and (b) shows the XRD pattern of the MoS2/SNC-T-t sample for T = 700, 800, 900, and 950 °C for t = 6 and 12 h. The data clearly shows that the height of the MoS2 diffraction peaks is insignificant when the PMS/SNC sample is treated at 700 °C for 12 h, indicating that the MoS2 is not fully formed. When the treatment temperature increases to 800 °C, diffraction peaks at 33.3 and 58.9° appear; these are assigned to the (100) and (110) diffraction peaks of hexagonal MoS2 (2H-MoS2, JCPDS 37-1492). This indicates that MoS2 forms at this temperature. Compared with bulk MoS2, no peak was observed at 2θ = 14.2°, which is attributed to the (002) diffraction peak and indicates that the material is highly oriented in the (100) direction. No (002) diffraction peak implies that the MoS2 has only a few layers owing to the PMS uniformly dispersing it in the carbon material matrix; this result agrees with the TEM results. Fig. 3(c) shows the XRD patterns of MoS2/SNC-900-12h prepared using various feeding amounts of Na2MoO4. The diffraction peaks of MoS2 became stronger and sharper with an increase in Na2MoO4, indicating that the size of MoS2 increases according to the Scherrer equation. Fig. 3(d) displays the Raman spectra of MoS2/SNC-900-12h and SNC-900-12h. Both the SNC-900-12h and MoS2/SNC-900-12h composites exhibit two dominant Raman peaks at 1341 and 1584 cm−1, respectively; these are a good match with the D and G bands of graphite, respectively. In general, the relative intensity ratio of the D and G bands, I D/I G, is an indication of the graphite quality, which means the delocalized π conjugation is partially restored during the hybridization process. The I D/I G value of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h hybrid (I D/I G = 0.99) is close to that of SNC-900-12h (I D/I G = 0.98), indicating that both samples have a similar degree of graphitization.

Elemental analysis of SNC-900-12h (Table 1) illustrates that some N and S are lost in the form of NH3 and H2S during the high temperature treatment. 3.3% N and 6.2% S, respectively, remained in SNC-900-12h to form N and S co-doped carbon materials. XPS was used to further confirm the composition and valance state of all elements in the composite. In Fig. 4(a) the XPS full-scan spectrum of SNC-900-12h shows that the material is mainly composed of C and O, while the S and N content is quite low, indicating that the S and N on the surface of the carbon material is lost during the high temperature treatment. For MoS2/SNC-900-12h, strong Mo and S signals can be observed at 234 and 163 eV, respectively. The N 1s XPS signal is still quite weak, though. Fig. 4(b) shows a high resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of SNC-900-12h, which has four different peaks corresponding to C=C/C–C (284.6 eV), C–C (sp 3, 284.9 eV), C–O (286.1 eV), and COOR (288.9 eV) groups. Fig. 4(c) shows a high resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of MoS2/SNC-900-12h, which has five different peaks corresponding to C=C/C–C (284.6 eV), C–C (sp 3, 284.9 eV), C–O (285.9 eV), and COOR (289.4 eV) groups; further, another peak is visible at 286.8 eV, which corresponds to C–S/C–N, indicating S and N doped graphite. High-resolution XPS data of the composite in the Mo 3d region is shown in Fig. 4(d). It can be deconvoluted into three peaks, one of which is at 226.8 eV; this one actually corresponds to the S 2s of MoS2. The two-main intense Mo 3d 5/2 (229.4 eV) and Mo 3d 3/2 (232.6 eV) components are characteristic of MoS2, while the high binding energy peak of Mo 3d (235.8 eV) corresponds to MoO3, which may result from the oxidation of the catalyst sample in air [25, 26]. The sulfur species were determined from the high-resolution XPS S 2p spectrum (Fig. 4(e)). The main doublet located at binding energies of 162.2 and 163.5 eV corresponds to the S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1/2 (C–S–C) lines of MoS2 [25]. Meanwhile, a peak at the binding energy at 164.9 eV suggests the existence of bridging disulfides S2 2– and/or apical S2– ligands (C=S), which may be related to high activity HER species [27]. The high-energy components at 168.1 and 169.2 eV can be assigned to high valance S species C–SO x –C [28,29]. Fig. 4(f) shows the high resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of MoS2/SNC-900-12h, which has three peaks at 395.5, 398.5, and 401.4 eV corresponding to Mo 3p 3/2 in MoS2, pyridinic, and graphitic N, respectively, which contribute to the electron density of the π-conjugated system via a pair of π-electrons in the graphitic carbon layers.

To investigate the catalytic activity of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite toward HER, LSVs of the various catalysts were measured in a N2 saturated H2SO4 solution (0.5 mol/L) using a typical three-electrode configuration. Commercial Pt/active carbon, bulk MoS2, and SNC-900-12h were used as references. The samples were deposited on a glassy carbon electrode (5 mm diameter) with a loading of 0.285 mg/cm2. Fig. 5(a) shows the HER polarization curves of 20% Pt/C, bulk MoS2, SNC-900-12h, and MoS2/SNC-900-12h. The bulk MoS2 and SNC-900-12h show much higher overpotentials. Fig. 5(c) shows the polarization curves of PMS/SNC treated at different temperatures for either 6 or 12 h. The data show that the electrocatalytic activity continuously improves after the thermal treatment. When PMS/SNC was treated at 800 °C for 6 h, the onset overpotential and the potential at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 were 165 and 322 mV vs. RHE, respectively. Both the onset potential and potential at 10 mA/cm2 reduce as the treatment temperature is increased. However, the electrocatalytic performance of MoS2/SNC-950-6h was decreased. From Fig. 5(a), it can be seen that the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite catalyst shows the smallest onset overpotential of 115 mV for hydrogen evolution, beyond which the cathodic current density rises rapidly under more negative potentials. The onset overpotential of MoS2/SNC-900-12h is lower than that of the other samples, indicating good catalytic activity of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite for HER. Moreover, the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite displayed the largest cathodic current density of all the test samples except for the commercial Pt/active carbon sample. MoS2/SNC-900-12h also exhibited the lowest overpotential (237 mV) at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. We also studied the effect of adding Na2MoO4 into the hydrothermal reaction. Fig. 5(d) shows the composite produced from adding between 0.5 and 2 mmol of Na2MoO4. When 1 mmol of Na2MoO4 was added into the reaction, MoS2/SNC-900-12h gave the best catalytic activity. A tafel plot obtained from the overpotential vs. the logarithm of the current density (η vs. log(j)) are shown in Fig. 5(b), which can be used to perform a quantitative kinetics analysis of HER [30]. The Tafel slopes can be determined by fitting the linear portions of the Tafel plots to the Tafel equation (η = blog(j) + a, where b is the Tafel slope). The values of the Tafel slope from Fig. 5(b) were 184, 151, 78, and 30 mA/decade for SNC-900-12h, bulk MoS2, MoS2/SNC-900-12h, and 20% Pt/C, respectively. Although the Tafel slopes of the MoS2/SCN-900-12h composites were lower than those of the MoS2/graphene catalyst [31], this may be because of some active sites were lost owing to the MoS2 embedded in the carbon matrix.

The interfacial reactions and electrode kinetics of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite catalyst in HER can be further demonstrated by EIS. The Nyquist plot of the samples and the corresponding electrical equivalent circuit diagram are given in Fig. 6(a) and (b), respectively, while the impedance parameters determined by fitting the EIS response are listed in Table 2 . The ohmic series resistance (R s) was determined via the intercept of the semicircle and the real axis, and the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and the corresponding interfacial capacitance were determined by fitting the semicircle of the Nyquist plot in terms of the equivalent circuit model shown in Fig. 6(b). The R s of all the samples falls within the range of 4–5 Ω, which is a manifestation of the good conductivity of the electrolyte. In addition, the charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the MoS2/SNC composites follow the sequence MoS2/SNC-900-12h (124 Ω) < bulk MoS2 (7.18 kΩ) < SNC-900-12h (9.88 kΩ), which is in accordance with the HER results shown in Fig. 5(a). We believe a synergistic effect between MoS2 and SNC promotes the change in conductivity. At the same time, MoS2 formation during the high temperature treatment may also enhance the graphitization and conductivity of SNC. It has been well established that R ct is related to the electrocatalysis kinetics and that a lower R ct value corresponds to a faster reaction rate. Thus, a fast charge transfer during the electrocatalytic reaction coupling with the large exposed active surface area of the catalyst likely contributes to the superior electrocatalytic activity of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite. Fig. 6(c) shows the Nyquist plots of PMS/SNC treated at different temperatures for either 6 or 12 h; the results are in accordance with the HER results shown in Fig. 5(c). Fig. 6(d) shows the Nyquist plots of the composite produced from adding between 0.5 and 2 mmol Na2MoO42H2O; the results are in accordance with the HER results shown in Fig. 5(d).

Furthermore, to probe the HER stability of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite, we cycled the catalyst continuously for 5000 cycles between 0 and −0.3 V vs. RHE at 100 mV/s. After cycling, the catalyst was measured at the same applied potential range as for the initial test. As shown in Fig. 7(a) , the current density of the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite catalyst had decreased slightly (∼2.6%) compared with the initial value (at a current density of 10 mA/cm2) after 5000 continuous cycles, indicating that the MoS2/SNC-900-12h composite catalyst exhibits excellent HER activity with long-term stability. We also characterized the sample after 5000 cycles; Fig. 7(b) shows that the XRD pattern of MoS2/SNC-900-12h after 5000 cycles. Another new peak was observed at 2θ = 16.5°, which corresponds to the (002) diffraction peak. This indicates that a small amount of MoS2 may aggregate during the test. Fig. 7(c) and (d) show the TEM and HRTEM images of MoS2/SNC-900-12h after 5000 test cycles. A small amount of multi-layer MoS2 was observed. Most of the MoS2 was still in a few-layers state, however, to which we attribute the excellent stability of MoS2/SNC-900-12h.

In summary, we developed a simple synthesis route to prepare graphite S, N co-doped carbon solid powder using a one-step hydrothermal method. SNC possesses a porous structure and multiple functional group because it is prepared using a bottom-up route. MoO4 2– was further introduced into the reaction to fabricate a MoS2/SNC composite, which can chemically bond with the functional groups on the carbon materials. This results in the MoO4 2– being uniformly dispersed into the carbon materials. A high temperature treatment helps MoO4 2– transfer into MoS2 since S2– can be released from SNC during the high temperature treatment. Only few-layered MoS2 can be formed during the thermal treatment because the MoO4 2– is embedded in the carbon materials. After treatment at 900 °C for 12 h, the MoS2/SNC-900-12h material exhibits good electrocatalytic properties with a small onset of the overpotential at 115 mV, a large cathodic current density, and small Tafel slope of 78 mV per decade. At the same time, the MoS2/SNC-900-12h exhibits excellent stability.
